Hunter — investigation log

Investigation log — env_004_cloud_identity_lotl

Briefing

Network topology: { "name": "Hybrid identity environment \u2014 corp.local / corp.com", "description": "A mid-sized organization with hybrid identity: an on-premises Active Directory domain (corp.local) \u2014 a perimeter firewall, a domain controller, and staff workstations \u2014 synced via Microsoft Entra Connect to a Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant (corp.com) comprising Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, and a GRC/ITSM platform used for change management and engagement records.", "current_date": "2026-06-12", "segments": [ { "id": "on-prem", "description": "On-premises corp.local Active Directory domain and LAN. Address range 10.10.0.0/16." }, { "id": "cloud", "description": "The corp.com Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant, hybrid-joined to corp.local via Microsoft Entra Connect." } ], "devices": [ { "id": "fw01", "hostname": "EDGE-FW01", "role": "perimeter firewall", "os": "vendor appliance (firewall/router)", "ip": "203.0.113.10", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Internet-facing edge device for the on-premises network. NATs outbound traffic and provides a VPN gateway for remote staff access to on-prem resources." }, { "id": "dc01", "hostname": "CORP-DC01", "role": "domain controller", "os": "Windows Server 2022", "ip": "10.10.0.10", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Primary Active Directory domain controller for corp.local. Also runs DNS for the internal network. Synced to the corp.com Microsoft Entra ID tenant via Microsoft Entra Connect (hybrid identity) \u2014 most on-prem accounts have a corresponding cloud identity." }, { "id": "ws01", "hostname": "IT-JDOE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.12", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Primary workstation for an IT administrator (jdoe) who handles day-to-day Microsoft 365 / Entra ID administration \u2014 app registrations, licensing, and access requests \u2014 alongside on-prem AD tasks." }, { "id": "entra01", "hostname": "corp.com (Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant)", "role": "cloud identity provider and M365 admin center", "os": "SaaS \u2014 Microsoft Entra ID", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Identity provider for the corp.com Microsoft 365 tenant, hybrid-joined to corp.local via Microsoft Entra Connect. Exposes interactive and service-principal sign-in logs, Entra ID audit logs (account, application, and consent changes), Identity Protection risk detections, conditional access policies, and the tenant's app/service-principal registry (registrations, delegated and application permission grants, consents). Roughly 150 user accounts, including staff, IT/admin accounts, service accounts, and a number of external/guest and contractor accounts (e.g. for auditors, vendors, and consultants) granted for the duration of their engagements." }, { "id": "exo01", "hostname": "corp.com (Exchange Online)", "role": "cloud email and calendaring", "os": "SaaS \u2014 Exchange Online", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Hosts mailboxes for all corp.com users, including the executive team and staff working on an active M&A workstream. Supports mailbox-level audit logging (e.g. MailItemsAccessed) and message tracing." }, { "id": "spo01", "hostname": "corp.com (SharePoint Online)", "role": "cloud document storage and collaboration", "os": "SaaS \u2014 SharePoint Online", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Hosts departmental and project SharePoint sites, including sites supporting the M&A workstream referenced above. Site-level access is logged via the SharePoint access/audit log." }, { "id": "itsm01", "hostname": "GRC / IT service management platform", "role": "change management, ticketing, and vendor/engagement records", "os": "SaaS \u2014 ITSM/GRC platform", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "System of record for change tickets, security engagement records (e.g. third-party assessments/penetration tests), and vendor/contractor access requests across both the on-prem and cloud environments." } ] }

You have up to 15 collect_evidence calls for this investigation. Begin.

Step 1

Initial hypotheses from topology

This is a hybrid identity environment with an active M&A workstream — a classic target for data theft and business-email-compromise. Let me enumerate hypotheses:

  1. Initial access via perimeter — VPN gateway on fw01 compromised (password spray, exposed mgmt interface, anomalous VPN logins from odd geos).
  2. Identity compromise in the cloud — risky/anomalous Entra sign-ins, MFA bypass, impossible travel, especially against admin (jdoe) or M&A-involved accounts.
  3. App-registration / OAuth consent abuse — malicious service principal or over-privileged app grant (a stealthy persistence path in M365).
  4. Privilege escalation / persistence in Entra — new admin role assignments, account creation, credential additions to service principals.
  5. Mailbox compromise / BEC — MailItemsAccessed on executive/M&A mailboxes, suspicious inbox rules, forwarding.
  6. SharePoint M&A data exfiltration — anomalous access to M&A sites, bulk downloads.
  7. On-prem AD compromise — DCSync, suspicious logons, lateral movement to DC01.
  8. Entra Connect abuse — the sync account is highly privileged; abuse of it bridges on-prem→cloud.
  9. Guest/contractor account abuse — engagement accounts overstaying or over-scoped.

Let me start broad across the cloud identity layer and perimeter, since those are the most likely entry/pivot points.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Show Entra ID Identity Protection risk detections and risky sign-ins over the last 30 days: include user, risk level, risk type (e.g. unfamiliar sign-in properties, impossible travel, anonymous IP), source IP/geo, and whether the risk was remediated or dismissed. Prioritize admin accounts, service principals, and accounts involved in the M&A workstream.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

List all Entra ID audit log events over the last 45 days for: directory role assignments (added member to admin role), user account creations, and credential changes to service principals/app registrations (new client secrets or certificates added). Include actor, target, timestamp, and source IP.

→ collect_evidence(fw01)

Show VPN gateway authentication logs for the last 30 days: successful and failed logins, with username, source public IP, geolocation, and timestamp. Flag any password-spray patterns (many failures across accounts) and any successful logins from previously unseen geographies.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: anomalous_interactive_signin, competing_pentest_pt2026_03, competing_spray

← result — found: True, note: EDGE-FW01 is the on-premises VPN gateway for corp.local domain accounts. Cloud-only accounts (including ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com, pentest-svc@corp.com) have no on-prem AD object and no VPN credential — their activity does not appear in this log regardless of volume.

(Entra ID / corp.com) on 2026-05-26 — see Entra ID Identity Protection

Rows returned: 214  |  Novel-geo flags: 4  |  Spray patterns: 0

Step 2

Very strong leads. Two distinct threads stand out:

Thread A — pentest-svc@corp.com: HIGH-risk impossible-travel, Tor, bulk Graph enumeration via "RedTeam-Recon-Tool" app. Name suggests an authorized penetration test. This is the classic "cited authorization is a claim, not proof" case — I must verify against the ITSM engagement records before concluding benign.

Thread B — ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com: Single-factor (legacy-auth exception), low-risk unfamiliar sign-in from Belgrade RS on 5/28, then at 23:31 UTC the same night registered "Mailbox Archiver Pro" — an unverified-publisher app with an external reply URI (mailbox-tools.net) from the same Belgrade IP. This pattern (auditor account → registers a mailbox-access OAuth app from a foreign IP) is highly suspicious of OAuth consent-grant abuse / mailbox exfiltration, especially given the active M&A workstream.

Let me pursue both. I need: (1) ITSM engagement records to validate/refute the pentest and the auditor engagement scope; (2) the OAuth permission grants/consents on the two suspect apps to understand actual capability.

→ collect_evidence(itsm01)

Retrieve all active and recent (last 90 days) third-party security-engagement and vendor/contractor access records, specifically: any authorized penetration test or red-team engagement (scope, authorized accounts/IPs, start/end dates, sign-off) that would cover the account 'pentest-svc@corp.com' and app 'RedTeam-Recon-Tool'; and the engagement/access record for the external auditor 'ext-auditor-rwilcox' (rwilcox) — scope, sponsor, authorized activities, start/end dates, and whether registering applications or accessing mailboxes is in scope.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

For the service principals 'Mailbox Archiver Pro' (appId 8f2c1e4a-91b6-4d2a-9e3f-7a1c5d6e2b90) and 'RedTeam-Recon-Tool' (appId c3f7a112-55d8-4b00-ae2c-1e9d8c4f6037): list all OAuth2 permission grants (delegated) and app-role assignments (application permissions) granted to each, who consented and when (admin vs user consent), and any client secrets/certificates added to them. Also report whether 'Expense Insights' (f9e8d7c6...) received any Graph/Exchange permission grants.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: account_provenance, competing_pentest_pt2026_03_auth

Step 3

The picture is now sharp. Resolving my hypotheses:

  • Pentest-svc / RedTeam-Recon-Tool: Fully corroborated as authorized — PT-2026-03, CISO-signed, window 6/08–6/10, scopes match exactly (Directory.Read.All, User.Read.All), short-lived secret, change ticket CHG-2026-0591. The HIGH alerts are expected red-team noise. REFUTED as malicious. (Minor hygiene: MFA exception IT-2026-0619 pending cleanup.)

  • ext-auditor-rwilcox / Mailbox Archiver Pro: CONFIRMED malicious/abuse. The auditor engagement (Hartwell, EXT-AUD-2025-02) closed 2025-12-18 with offboarding never completed — account still enabled, single-factor MFA exception live. The account then, ~5 months post-engagement, signed in from Belgrade RS and registered an unverified-publisher app with an external reply URI on mailbox-tools.net, self-consented Mail.Read + Files.Read.All, all from the foreign IP, with no change ticket and no authorization — explicitly out of original scope. This is OAuth-app-based mailbox/file exfiltration persistence.

Critical next step: determine whether this app was actually used to read mail/files — the difference between "registered capability" and "active exfiltration." I should check Exchange Online (MailItemsAccessed by this app/principal) and SharePoint access by this account, focused on M&A resources. I'll also pull the full sign-in history for rwilcox to scope the dormant-then-active timeline.

→ collect_evidence(exo01)

Search the unified/mailbox audit log for any MailItemsAccessed, Bind, or message-read operations performed via appId 8f2c1e4a-91b6-4d2a-9e3f-7a1c5d6e2b90 ('Mailbox Archiver Pro') or by principal ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com, from 2026-05-28 to present. For each, list the accessing app/principal, target mailbox, folders/items accessed, count, and client IP. Highlight any access to executive or M&A-workstream mailboxes (e.g. t.vance, d.koenig, s.aldridge).

→ collect_evidence(spo01)

Pull SharePoint Online access/audit log entries for principal ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com and for appId 8f2c1e4a-91b6-4d2a-9e3f-7a1c5d6e2b90 ('Mailbox Archiver Pro') from 2026-01-01 to present. List sites accessed, files viewed/downloaded, operation type, client IP, and timestamps. Specifically flag any access to M&A-workstream sites or sites outside the Finance Reporting site.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Provide the full interactive and non-interactive sign-in history for ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com from 2025-12-18 to 2026-06-12: timestamps, source IP, geo, application/client, auth method, MFA status, and success/failure. I want to establish whether the account was dormant after engagement close and when it became active again, and from where.

← result — found: True, note: MailItemsAccessed auditing was not enabled tenant-wide prior to 2026-06-01. No mailbox-audit records of any kind are available for the window 2026-05-28 through 2026-05-31 for any principal or application. This is a logging-coverage gap from the phased Purview audit rollout, not a data-deletion or tampering event.

🎯 embeds ground truth: sp_data_access, evidence_gap_mailitemsaccessed, decoy_corpvault_backup, decoy_onna_ediscovery

← result — found: True, note: Sign-in log live retention is 30 days for interactive and non-interactive logs. Records for 2025-12-18 through 2025-12-19 are available from the 90-day Entra ID audit export archive. Records for the intervening dormancy period (2025-12-20 through 2026-05-12) have aged out of all available retention windows and cannot be retrieved; however, the Identity Protection and audit export records confirm zero authentication events in that period within their own retention scopes.

🎯 embeds ground truth: anomalous_interactive_signin, account_provenance, oauth_app_registration, oauth_consent_grant, competing_spray

  (interactive + portal)

END OF RESULTS
========================================

Step 4

The case is fully built. This is active, ongoing exfiltration of M&A-sensitive data via a malicious OAuth application, leveraging a stale, MFA-exempt contractor account.

Timeline of compromise: 1. The Hartwell auditor account (ext-auditor-rwilcox) was never offboarded after engagement close 2025-12-18 — stayed enabled, licensed, and MFA-exempt (IT-2025-0734, no expiry). 2. ~5 months dormant, then on 2026-05-28 23:14 UTC signed in from Belgrade, RS (residential-proxy ASN, never seen before, single-factor — no MFA challenge because of the stale exception). Just two days after the 5/26 password-spray that targeted this account, suggesting credential compromise as the entry vector. 3. In a single 60-minute session it registered "Mailbox Archiver Pro" (unverified publisher, external reply URI mailbox-tools.net), self-consented Mail.Read + Files.Read.All, and added a client secret — establishing app-based persistence that survives the user session. 4. The service principal then ran daily, automated (python-requests, Azure Graph IPs) collection: - Mailboxes: CEO (t.vance), CFO (s.aldridge), M&A lead (d.koenig) — 440 items, 6/01–6/11 (and likely 5/28–5/31, in the MailItemsAccessed logging gap). - SharePoint Project Atlas (Confidential M&A site): 55+ files 5/30–6/11 — term sheets, financing models, LOI/SPA drafts, due-diligence trackers, valuations, board resolutions. - The app had no membership/permission on Project Atlas — it reached the site purely via the Files.Read.All delegated grant.

This is out of scope for the original engagement, unauthorized (no change ticket anywhere), and ongoing as of the present date. The app and its secret are still active.

Hypothesis resolution: H5/H6 (mailbox + SharePoint exfil) and H3 (OAuth abuse) and H9 (stale contractor) all confirmed and linked. H1 (VPN) refuted. H2 — the only residual is whether the 5/26 spray actually yielded rwilcox's password (can't confirm vs. other vector). H7/H8 (on-prem AD / Entra Connect) untested — carrying to open questions. The pentest thread (RedTeam-Recon-Tool) is refuted as malicious (authorized PT-2026-03).

Final report

Summary: A stale, MFA-exempt external-auditor account (ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com, never offboarded after its FY2025 engagement closed 2025-12-18) was used on 2026-05-28 from a Belgrade, RS proxy to register a malicious OAuth application ("Mailbox Archiver Pro"), which has since conducted daily automated exfiltration of executive/M&A mailbox content and confidential M&A SharePoint documents. The activity is unauthorized, out of scope, and ongoing as of the investigation date. A separate cluster of high-risk Entra alerts traces to an authorized, CISO-signed penetration test and is benign.

Findings

1. Malicious OAuth app 'Mailbox Archiver Pro' exfiltrating executive/M&A mail and confidential M&A documents (active)

  • Affected devices: entra01, exo01, spo01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: critical
  • Evidence: Entra audit (C-002/C-003) shows ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com registered app 'Mailbox Archiver Pro' (appId 8f2c1e4a-91b6-4d2a-9e3f-7a1c5d6e2b90) at 2026-05-28 23:31 UTC from Belgrade IP 188.43.112.77 — unverified publisher, external reply URI https://archiver-pro.mailbox-tools.net/auth/callback. The OAuth grant inventory shows self-consented delegated Mail.Read + Files.Read.All and a client secret created the same minute; no admin oversight, no app access policy. Exchange Online unified audit log shows the SP (user agent python-requests/2.31.0, Azure Graph IPs 40.99.14.x) accessed mailboxes of t.vance (CEO, 174 items), s.aldridge (CFO, 121), and d.koenig (M&A lead, 145) — 440 items total, daily 2026-06-01 through 06-11 (earlier days fall in a MailItemsAccessed logging gap). SharePoint audit shows the same SP accessed the Confidential, restricted-membership M&A site /sites/ProjectAtlas daily 2026-05-30 through 06-11, reading 55+ files (term sheets, financing models, LOI/SPA drafts, due-diligence trackers, valuations, board resolutions) despite having no membership/permission on the site — access obtained purely via the Files.Read.All grant. The app, its secret, and the grants remain Active. ITSM returned NO change ticket or authorization for this app or any post-2025-12-18 activity by this account.

2. Stale external-auditor account never offboarded; permanent MFA exception enabled the compromise

  • Affected devices: entra01, itsm01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: high
  • Evidence: ITSM engagement EXT-AUD-2025-02 (Hartwell & Associates) closed 2025-12-18 with offboarding actions required (disable account, reclaim license, remove MFA exception) but 'NO RECORD FOUND' — no deprovision ticket, account still enabled and E3-licensed, and legacy-auth MFA exception IT-2025-0734 still active with no expiry. Sign-in history shows the account dormant ~160 days (2025-12-19 to 2026-05-28), then a single interactive sign-in 2026-05-28 23:14 UTC from Belgrade, RS (188.43.112.77, residential-proxy ASN, never-before-seen geo/device) completed with single-factor password only because the MFA exception suppressed the CA MFA-All-Users policy (Identity Protection flagged it merely LOW). That 60-minute session performed all the app-registration and consent actions. The dormant, over-retained, MFA-exempt account was the enabling weakness for the entire intrusion.

3. Possible credential-compromise vector: password spray two days before first malicious sign-in

  • Affected devices: entra01
  • Confidence: medium — Severity: high
  • Evidence: Identity Protection cluster IPD-20260526-0001..0005 records a low-and-slow password spray (~600 failures / ~80 accounts) from 45.142.0.0/24 on 2026-05-26, including 4 failed attempts against ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com. The account's first malicious sign-in followed ~2 days later from an unrelated Belgrade proxy. Causation is not proven — the successful sign-in used a single correct password and the spray attempts on this account did not lock out — but the timing and the account's single-factor exposure make spray (or prior credential theft) the most plausible entry vector. No remediation/dismissal was actioned on any spray alert.

4. High-severity Entra risk alerts from 'pentest-svc'/'RedTeam-Recon-Tool' are authorized penetration-test activity (benign)

  • Affected devices: entra01, itsm01
  • Confidence: high — Severity: low
  • Evidence: Multiple HIGH Identity Protection alerts (impossible travel, Tor, bulk Graph enumeration) for pentest-svc@corp.com / RedTeam-Recon-Tool (appId c3f7a112...) match ITSM engagement PT-2026-03 (Vantage Assurance Partners): CISO digitally signed 2026-06-05, authorized window 2026-06-08–06-10, scope explicitly includes directory enumeration and OAuth/app testing via Graph and notes rotating exit nodes (SOW 4.2). Pre-registered account and app, change ticket CHG-2026-0591. Granted scopes (Directory.Read.All, User.Read.All) match the authorized scope exactly; secret is short-lived (30-day). Risk alerts are expected red-team noise. Note: this app is a benign look-alike to the malicious one and must NOT be conflated — different appId, actor, scopes, and full authorization. Minor hygiene gap: MFA exception IT-2026-0619 pending cleanup.

Open questions

  • Immediate containment (recommended): revoke 'Mailbox Archiver Pro' service principal, secret, and OAuth grants; disable ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com and revoke its refresh tokens; remove MFA exception IT-2025-0734. Then audit all other accounts on legacy-auth/MFA exceptions for similar staleness.
  • Scope of data actually exfiltrated during the MailItemsAccessed logging gap (2026-05-28 to 05-31) cannot be reconstructed from the mailbox audit log; SharePoint shows Project Atlas access began 2026-05-30, so mailbox collection likely also started before 06-01. Quantify exposure assuming the full 05-28→present window.
  • Confirm the entry vector for the 2026-05-28 sign-in — was rwilcox's password obtained via the 2026-05-26 spray, prior breach, or phishing? Check the Hartwell side and any token/credential reuse.
  • Investigate the destination infrastructure: reply URI domain mailbox-tools.net (and archiver-pro subdomain) — WHOIS/passive DNS/threat-intel to attribute the actor and check for other victim tenants using the same OAuth-app pattern.
  • Did the actor establish any additional persistence (other app registrations, inbox/forwarding rules on the three targeted mailboxes, added secrets/certs elsewhere)? Pull inbox-rule and transport-rule changes for t.vance, s.aldridge, d.koenig.
  • Untested hypotheses carried forward: on-prem AD compromise on dc01 (DCSync / suspicious privileged logons) and abuse of the Entra Connect sync account — not examined this engagement; recommend reviewing given the bridge between corp.local and corp.com.
  • Verify 'Expense Insights' (jdoe-registered, admin-consented Mail.Read but App Access Policy-scoped to receipts@corp.com, ticket CHG-2026-0608): confirm the access policy is effective and the unverified-publisher app is the intended Finance pilot vendor, not a typosquat.

Hunter — no-incident control

Investigation log — env_004_cloud_identity_lotl_control

Briefing

Network topology: { "name": "Hybrid identity environment \u2014 corp.local / corp.com", "description": "A mid-sized organization with hybrid identity: an on-premises Active Directory domain (corp.local) \u2014 a perimeter firewall, a domain controller, and staff workstations \u2014 synced via Microsoft Entra Connect to a Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant (corp.com) comprising Exchange Online, SharePoint Online, and a GRC/ITSM platform used for change management and engagement records.", "current_date": "2026-06-12", "segments": [ { "id": "on-prem", "description": "On-premises corp.local Active Directory domain and LAN. Address range 10.10.0.0/16." }, { "id": "cloud", "description": "The corp.com Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant, hybrid-joined to corp.local via Microsoft Entra Connect." } ], "devices": [ { "id": "fw01", "hostname": "EDGE-FW01", "role": "perimeter firewall", "os": "vendor appliance (firewall/router)", "ip": "203.0.113.10", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Internet-facing edge device for the on-premises network. NATs outbound traffic and provides a VPN gateway for remote staff access to on-prem resources." }, { "id": "dc01", "hostname": "CORP-DC01", "role": "domain controller", "os": "Windows Server 2022", "ip": "10.10.0.10", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Primary Active Directory domain controller for corp.local. Also runs DNS for the internal network. Synced to the corp.com Microsoft Entra ID tenant via Microsoft Entra Connect (hybrid identity) \u2014 most on-prem accounts have a corresponding cloud identity." }, { "id": "ws01", "hostname": "IT-JDOE", "role": "workstation", "os": "Windows 11", "ip": "10.10.10.12", "segment": "on-prem", "notes": "Primary workstation for an IT administrator (jdoe) who handles day-to-day Microsoft 365 / Entra ID administration \u2014 app registrations, licensing, and access requests \u2014 alongside on-prem AD tasks." }, { "id": "entra01", "hostname": "corp.com (Microsoft Entra ID / Microsoft 365 tenant)", "role": "cloud identity provider and M365 admin center", "os": "SaaS \u2014 Microsoft Entra ID", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Identity provider for the corp.com Microsoft 365 tenant, hybrid-joined to corp.local via Microsoft Entra Connect. Exposes interactive and service-principal sign-in logs, Entra ID audit logs (account, application, and consent changes), Identity Protection risk detections, conditional access policies, and the tenant's app/service-principal registry (registrations, delegated and application permission grants, consents). Roughly 150 user accounts, including staff, IT/admin accounts, service accounts, and a number of external/guest and contractor accounts (e.g. for auditors, vendors, and consultants) granted for the duration of their engagements." }, { "id": "exo01", "hostname": "corp.com (Exchange Online)", "role": "cloud email and calendaring", "os": "SaaS \u2014 Exchange Online", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Hosts mailboxes for all corp.com users, including the executive team and staff working on an active M&A workstream. Supports mailbox-level audit logging (e.g. MailItemsAccessed) and message tracing." }, { "id": "spo01", "hostname": "corp.com (SharePoint Online)", "role": "cloud document storage and collaboration", "os": "SaaS \u2014 SharePoint Online", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "Hosts departmental and project SharePoint sites, including sites supporting the M&A workstream referenced above. Site-level access is logged via the SharePoint access/audit log." }, { "id": "itsm01", "hostname": "GRC / IT service management platform", "role": "change management, ticketing, and vendor/engagement records", "os": "SaaS \u2014 ITSM/GRC platform", "ip": "n/a \u2014 cloud service", "segment": "cloud", "notes": "System of record for change tickets, security engagement records (e.g. third-party assessments/penetration tests), and vendor/contractor access requests across both the on-prem and cloud environments." } ] }

You have up to 15 collect_evidence calls for this investigation. Begin.

Step 1

Initial hypothesis list

This is a hybrid identity environment with an active M&A workstream — a high-value target for espionage/BEC. Key hypotheses:

  1. Cloud identity attack / illicit consent grant — attacker abuses Entra ID via OAuth app consent, service principal credential addition, or risky sign-ins to reach M365 data.
  2. Credential access / privilege escalation on-prem (DC01) — DCSync, suspicious admin logons, account creation.
  3. Initial access via VPN / firewall — anomalous remote logins through fw01, especially from foreign IPs.
  4. M&A data targeting — anomalous mailbox access (MailItemsAccessed) on executive/M&A mailboxes, or SharePoint M&A site access.
  5. Compromise of the IT admin workstation (ws01/jdoe) — jdoe holds M365 admin rights; a prime target for token theft / lateral pivot to cloud.
  6. Guest/contractor account abuse — the tenant has many external accounts; one could be over-permissioned or attacker-controlled.

Let me start broad across the cloud identity plane and the perimeter, since those are the likely entry and pivot points.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Show Entra ID audit log entries for the last 30 days related to application/service-principal changes: new app registrations, service principal creations, addition of credentials (client secrets or certificates) to existing service principals/apps, and OAuth2 permission/consent grants (delegated and application). Include timestamp, actor (user or SP), target app/SP name and appId, and the permissions granted.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Show Identity Protection risk detections and risky sign-ins for the last 30 days: anonymous IP / Tor, unfamiliar sign-in properties, impossible travel, malware-linked IPs, and any successful interactive sign-ins from foreign or hosting-provider IP ranges. Include account, timestamp, source IP, ASN/geo, app, and MFA result.

→ collect_evidence(fw01)

Show VPN gateway authentication logs for the last 30 days: successful and failed remote-access logins with username, source public IP, geo/ASN, and session duration. Flag any logins from new or foreign source IPs.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: competing_pentest_pt2026_03, decoy_expense_insights, decoy_corpvault_backup, decoy_onna_ediscovery, decoy_hubspot_connector, decoy_powerbi_finance

               hit smart-lockout threshold

EDGE-FW01 — VPN Gateway Authentication Log
Query window: 2026-05-13 00:00:00 UTC → 2026-06-12 23:59:59 UTC
Log source: /var/log/vpn/auth.log (IKEv2/SSL-VPN gateway)
Filter: All authentication events (SUCCESS + FAILURE)
Returned: 214 events

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SECTION A — FAILED AUTHENTICATION ATTEMPTS
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

── Burst: 2026-05-26 18:02–23:48 UTC ──────────────────────────────────────
  Note: High-volume failure burst coinciding with broader auth activity
  against corp.com tenant observed during this window.

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
SUMMARY
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
  Successful VPN sessions (30d)           : 47
  Failed auth attempts (30d)              : ~62 total (57 in spray burst; 5 routine)
  VPN lockouts (30d)                      : 2 (jdoe, b.hartmann — both from spray burst)
  Flagged new/foreign-IP successes        : 5 (all MFA-satisfied staff)
  Successful auths from 45.142.0.0/23     : 0
  Accounts with zero VPN activity (30d)   : ext-auditor-rwilcox (cloud-only acct — no
                                            VPN profile provisioned; not expected)
  VPN protocol                            : IKEv2 (primary) / SSL-VPN (fallback)
  MFA enforcement                         : Required for all non-LAN source IPs

Step 2

This is rich. Key observations:

  1. Password spray (2026-05-26) from AS206728 bulletproof hosting — ~600 cloud + ~57 VPN failed attempts, zero successes. Real but blocked. Notable: it's unactioned (alerts still "New").

  2. The pentest cluster (PT-2026-03) centered on pentest-svc@corp.com and RedTeam-Recon-Tool SP — Tor/Hetzner/M247/Hostinger IPs, bulk Graph directory enumeration. This is claimed to be an authorized pentest. But I must verify the engagement record rather than trust the annotations. Several things deserve scrutiny: - The RedTeam-Recon-Tool was registered by jdoe (10.10.10.12) on 06-05, but then on 06-08 pentest-svc re-registered/took ownership from 185.220.101.47 (Tor exit) and self-consented. The secret jdoe created expires 06-12; pentest activity continued through 06-10. - Critical mismatch: the original short-lived secret note says "PT-2026-03 engagement." I need to confirm PT-2026-03 exists, its authorized scope, source IPs, and dates — and whether pentest-svc is the legitimate engagement account. - The 185.220.101.47 Tor exit is the same IP that took ownership of the app — and the password spray IPs are different (45.142.x). Don't conflate.

  3. Expense Insights — unverified publisher app by jdoe, but scoped tightly to receipts@corp.com (Mail.Read). Plausibly benign.

Let me verify the pentest authorization at the system of record, and check whether pentest-svc went beyond directory recon into the M&A data (the actual crown jewels). Also verify pentest-svc's account provenance in Entra.

→ collect_evidence(itsm01)

Pull the full engagement/change record for "PT-2026-03" (penetration test) and any related vendor/contractor access request: authorized scope, rules of engagement, approved testing source IPs, start/end dates and time windows, the vendor/firm name, the named tester account(s) (e.g. pentest-svc@corp.com), approver, and authorization status. Also list any other open security engagement or vendor access records active in May–June 2026.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Provide the full account provenance and configuration for pentest-svc@corp.com: creation timestamp and creator, account type (member/guest/cloud-only vs synced), assigned directory roles and group memberships, owned/assigned applications and service principals, MFA registration details and when registered, and a complete list of its sign-in events (interactive and non-interactive) in the last 30 days with source IP, app, and result. Also show who owns the RedTeam-Recon-Tool app (8b2e5f14-...) currently and the ownership-change history.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: competing_pentest_pt2026_03, competing_pentest_pt2026_03_auth, decoy_expense_insights, decoy_onna_ediscovery

── Vendor / Firm ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Vendor name      : Vantage Assurance Partners

── Scope ─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Scope            : corp.com Microsoft Entra ID tenant — external attack surface
                   and directory enumeration only.
                   Explicitly OUT OF SCOPE: on-premises corp.local domain and
                   all on-prem hosts; Exchange Online mailbox content; SharePoint
                   site content; any exfiltration or data-staging activity;
                   production data of any kind.
Methodology      : Black-box external, credential-based directory enumeration;
                   no social engineering; no phishing; no lateral movement into
                   on-prem or cloud workloads beyond directory read operations.

Authorized start : 2026-06-08 00:00:00 UTC

══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

Step 3

The pentest checks out thoroughly against the system of record: PT-2026-03 is CISO-approved (APR-2026-0441, CHG-2026-0587), authorized window 06-08 to 06-10, pre-declared source ranges that match exactly (185.220.101.0/24, 91.108.56.0/24, 45.83.122.0/24), account created by jdoe from the IT workstation, MFA registered internally, all activity scoped to Microsoft Graph Directory.Read.All/User.Read.All — no Exchange, SharePoint, or Teams tokens, all within the window, secrets cleaned up at close. The "Tor exit / impossible travel" alerts are exactly what an authorized external cloud pentest produces. This thread is refuted as malicious — it's legitimate, though Identity Protection alerts being left unactioned and the deprovisioning being overdue are hygiene issues.

Now let me resolve the remaining hypotheses. The key untested high-value targets are: - M&A data (Project Atlas) — the actual crown jewels. Did anyone anomalously access t.vance / d.koenig mailboxes or the M&A SharePoint site? Note Onna eDiscovery (legal hold LH-2026-014) legitimately accesses those mailboxes — I must distinguish that from anything else. - The stale ext-auditor-rwilcox account — overdue deprovisioning (closed Dec 2025), still enabled, and it was a spray target. Need to confirm zero successful use. - On-prem DC01 — entirely untested. DCSync, suspicious admin logons, account creation.

Let me pull these in parallel.

→ collect_evidence(exo01)

For the M&A-covered mailboxes t.vance@corp.com and d.koenig@corp.com, show mailbox audit log (MailItemsAccessed, FolderBind, Send, MoveToDeletedItems, and any mailbox delegation/Add-MailboxPermission or inbox-rule creation events) for the last 30 days. For each access, include timestamp, the accessing user/app identity (UPN or service principal/appId), client/auth type, source IP, and whether it was owner vs delegate/app access. Flag any access by identities other than the mailbox owner, the Onna eDiscovery app (2d7f4a93-...), or normal Outlook/mobile clients.

→ collect_evidence(spo01)

For the Project Atlas / M&A SharePoint site(s), show the access/audit log for the last 30 days: file views, downloads, syncs, sharing-link creation, and permission/access grants. Include timestamp, user or app identity (UPN or appId), operation, source IP, and file/document. Flag any access by external/guest accounts (e.g. ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com), by service principals, or any anomalous bulk-download activity.

→ collect_evidence(dc01)

Pull Security event log evidence for the last 30 days on CORP-DC01: (1) DCSync-style directory replication events — Event 4662 with replication GUIDs (DS-Replication-Get-Changes / -All) by non-DC accounts; (2) account creation/privilege changes — Events 4720, 4728, 4732, 4756 (additions to Domain Admins / Enterprise Admins / privileged groups); (3) interactive and RemoteInteractive logons (4624 type 2/10) by privileged/admin accounts with source IP; and (4) any Entra Connect / AAD sync account anomalies. Flag anything outside normal admin baseline.

← result — found: True

Query window : 2026-05-13 → 2026-06-12 | Host: CORP-DC01.corp.local

Step 4

All three high-value targets came back clean: - M&A mailboxes (t.vance, d.koenig): only owner access + Onna eDiscovery (LH-2026-014) + CorpVault Backup. Zero anomalous access, no inbox rules, no delegation. The M&A SharePoint site (Project Atlas) is restricted, external sharing disabled, no guest/SP access, no bulk download, no sharing links. - DC01: no DCSync, no privileged group changes, no off-hours/foreign admin logons, healthy Entra Connect. The two new accounts are routine IT provisioning by jdoe from the workstation. - The pentest identities and ext-auditor-rwilcox touched none of the crown-jewel data.

I've now resolved nearly every hypothesis. Let me account for the list:

  1. Cloud identity / illicit consent — refuted: all app/SP changes trace to jdoe from the IT workstation or the authorized pentest; tightly scoped; no anomalous consent.
  2. On-prem credential access / privesc — refuted: DC01 clean.
  3. VPN/firewall initial access — refuted as successful compromise: spray hit VPN but zero successes; lockouts triggered.
  4. M&A data targeting — refuted: no anomalous mailbox/SharePoint access.
  5. ws01/jdoe compromise — partially addressed: all admin actions originate from 10.10.10.12 with consistent Kerberos/business-hours patterns; no foreign jdoe logons. I haven't pulled endpoint/EDR from ws01 directly, so I'll note residual uncertainty.
  6. Guest/contractor abuse — mostly refuted: ext-auditor-rwilcox is a stale/overdue account but has zero successful sign-ins anywhere.

The findings that remain are hygiene/risk-exposure issues, not active compromise: the unactioned password-spray alerts, the overdue deprovisioning of ext-auditor-rwilcox (an enabled, unused, but orphaned cloud account that was a spray target — real attack surface), and the pending pentest cleanup. One remaining check worth doing within budget: confirm ext-auditor-rwilcox has no lingering standing access/permissions (its original scope was Finance SharePoint + shared mailboxes) — an enabled orphaned account with leftover grants is the most material exposure here.

→ collect_evidence(entra01)

Full configuration and risk posture for ext-auditor-rwilcox@corp.com: account enabled status, last sign-in (interactive and non-interactive) ever recorded and its date, UserType (guest/member), MFA registration state, assigned directory roles, group memberships, any current app/SharePoint/mailbox permission grants or standing access still attached to this account, and any credential/password/MFA-method changes in the last 90 days. Confirm whether the account has any successful authentication at any point since 2025-12-19.

← result — found: True

🎯 embeds ground truth: competing_spray, competing_spray_zero_success

                 (90 days) for credential/config changes; all-time for
                 last-sign-in metadata fields

  - Phone/SMS (+44 7xxx xxxxxx — number redacted) | Registered: 2025-10-01

   on 2025-10-01. No admin-initiated MFA reset events recorded.]
